title: Echocardiography before non-cardiac surgery: current knowledge, guideline recommendations, and clinical evidence – a narrative review
authors: Viktoria Mertin, Alexandra Stroda, Tobias Bruns, Giovanna Lurati Buse
journal: Journal of Clinical Monitoring and Computing
one_line_summary: This review highlights that while preoperative TTE/FOCUS can detect new cardiac pathologies in non-elective non-cardiac surgery, routine use lacks evidence for improved clinical outcomes, and guideline adherence remains poor.
key_pearls: Avoid routine preoperative TTE in asymptomatic, stable patients.
Utilize FOCUS as a targeted tool for specific clinical questions (e.g., suspected heart failure or valvular disease) to prevent surgical delays.
Prioritize clinical assessment (symptoms, functional capacity, biomarkers like NT-proBNP) over indiscriminate imaging.
Future practice should focus on identifying high-yield patient populations (e.g., those with severe valvular or ventricular dysfunction) rather than broad screening.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Yield
sections.content: Focused cardiac ultrasound (FOCUS) is highly effective in non-elective settings for detecting previously unknown left ventricular (LV) dysfunction, valvular pathology, and volume status abnormalities. It frequently helps 'downgrade' patients suspected of having cardiac disease who are found to have normal cardiac function.

sections.heading: Management Impact
sections.content: FOCUS findings lead to perioperative management changes (e.g., hemodynamic monitoring, anesthesia technique, or postoperative disposition) in 12–82% of cases, though the quality of this evidence is limited.

sections.heading: Outcome Impact
sections.content: Large administrative database studies have paradoxically associated preoperative TTE with worse outcomes (e.g., higher 30-day mortality), likely due to residual confounding (sicker patients receive more testing). Randomized controlled trials (e.g., PRE-OPFOCUS) have failed to show mortality or morbidity benefits from systematic FOCUS.

sections.heading: Guideline Recommendations (ESC 2022)
sections.content: The ESC 2022 guidelines provide specific recommendations for preoperative echocardiography: - Class I: Symptomatic patients, genetic cardiomyopathy, high-risk non-cardiac surgery (NCS) with poor functional capacity/high NT-proBNP. - Class IIa: New murmurs (intermediate risk), suspected new cardiovascular disease (CVD). - Class IIb: Intermediate-risk NCS with poor functional capacity/high NT-proBNP, FOCUS by trained personnel to avoid delay.

sections.heading: Guideline Recommendations (AHA/ACC 2024)
sections.content: The AHA/ACC 2024 guidelines also offer specific guidance: - Class I: New dyspnea, physical exam findings of heart failure, suspected severe valvular disease. - Class IIa: Dyspnea of unknown origin, known heart failure with worsening status. - Class IIb: Known LV dysfunction (stable), previously documented LV dysfunction without TTE in >12 months. - Class III: Routine evaluation in asymptomatic, stable patients.

strengths_limitations: The review highlights a critical lack of high-quality randomized data, noting that current guidelines are based on expert consensus rather than robust outcome-based evidence. It points out that 'over-testing' does not correlate with improved outcomes and strains limited echocardiography resources.
